questionText: In an examination, 35% candidates failed in one subject and 42% failed in another subject while 15% failed in both the subjects. If 2500 candidates appeared at the examination, how many passed in either subject but not in both?

steps.explanation: Find percentage who failed in at least one subject using inclusion-exclusion.
steps.formulaExpression: P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)
steps.result: 62% failed in at least one subject

steps.explanation: Find number who passed in both subjects (did not fail in any).
Find those who failed in exactly one subject (failed one but not both): these passed in exactly one subject.
steps.result: 1175 passed in either subject but not in both

solution.shortcut: Passed in exactly one = (failed only one subject) = [(A only) + (B only)] as % of total = (35-15+42-15)% of 2500 = 47% of 2500 = 1175
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 'passed in either but not both' with total passed. The question asks those who passed exactly one subject.

formulas.title: Inclusion-Exclusion
formulas.expression: P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)

questionText: Arnav ordered 4 pairs of black socks and some additional pairs of blue socks. The price of the black socks per pair was twice that of the blue socks. When the order was filled, it was found that the number of pairs of the two colours had been interchanged. This increased the bill by 50%. The ratio of the number of pairs of black socks to the number of pairs of blue socks in the original order was

steps.explanation: Let n = number of pairs of blue socks ordered, price of blue socks per pair = x. Then price of black socks per pair = 2x.
After interchange: n pairs of black socks at 2x each and 4 pairs of blue socks at x each.
The bill increased by 50%, so interchanged bill = 150% of actual bill.

solution.shortcut: Set up bill equation and use 150% condition to find n directly.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that black socks cost 2x and confusing which price applies to which colour after interchange.

questionText: A man ordered a length of rope by telephone from his nearest hardware shop. But when a worker in the shop brought the rope, he found that the man on the telephone had miswritten the order by interchanging feet and inches. As a result of this, the length of rope received was only 30% of the length he had ordered. The length of the rope which the man ordered was between

steps.explanation: Let the man order x feet y inches. Since x and y are in valid range, both are less than 12.
After miswriting, y feet x inches was received.
Since y < 12 and x < 12, and x = 4.5y, try y = 2: x = 9. Order = 9 ft 2 in ≈ 9.17 ft. Between 9 ft and 10.5 ft.
steps.calculation: y=2, x=9: order = 9 ft 2 in; y=1, x=4.5 (not integer); only y=2 gives valid integers.
steps.result: Order is 9 ft 2 in, between 9 ft and 10.5 ft

solution.shortcut: Convert to inches, set up 30% equation, solve ratio x:y = 9:2, find valid integer pair.
solution.commonMistake: Not remembering that both x and y must be less than 12 (valid feet/inches values).

questionText: A gardener has supply of fertilizer A which consists of 10% nitrogen and 6% phosphoric acid and fertilizer B which consists of 5% nitrogen and 10% phosphoric acid. After testing the soil conditions, he finds that he needs at least 14 kg of nitrogen and 14 kg of phosphoric acid for his crop. If fertilizer A costs ₹10.60 per kg and fertilizer B costs ₹8.40 per kg, what is the minimum cost at which the farmer can meet the nutrient requirement by using a combination of both types of fertilizers?

steps.explanation: Let x kg of A and y kg of B be used. Set up nitrogen equation.
Subtract (ii) from (i) to find ratio of x to y.
Substitute x = 5y/4 into (i) to find y.

solution.shortcut: Solve the 2-variable linear system to find exact kg required, then compute cost.
solution.commonMistake: Using only one nutrient constraint instead of both simultaneously.

questionText: I bought 5 pens, 7 pencils and 4 erasers. Rajan bought 6 pens, 8 erasers and 14 pencils for an amount which was half more than that I had paid. What percent of the total amount paid by me was paid for the pen?

steps.explanation: Let cost of 1 pen = x, 1 pencil = y, 1 eraser = z. My amount = p.
Rajan paid half more than me, i.e., 1.5p.
Multiply equation (i) by 2 and subtract (ii) from it.
Cost of 5 pens = 5x = 5 × 0.125p = 0.625p. Percentage of total amount paid for pens:

solution.shortcut: Multiply first equation by 2 and subtract second to eliminate pencils and erasers, isolating pen cost.
solution.commonMistake: Trying to solve for individual values of x, y, z instead of eliminating variables.

questionText: The majority against a certain motion is equal to 8% of the total number of voting. If 14 of those who voted against it had voted for it, the motion would have been carried by 4 votes. Find the number of votes for and against the motion.

steps.explanation: Let votes for = F, votes against = A, total = x. Majority against = A - F = 8% of x.
If 14 who voted against had voted for: new for = F+14, new against = A-14. Motion carried by 4 means (F+14) - (A-14) = 4.
From (i): 8% of x = 24 → x = 300. Total votes = 300.
A - F = 24 and A + F = 300. Solve: A = 162, F = 138.

solution.shortcut: Find the majority (A-F) from the transfer condition, then use 8% to find total votes, then split.
solution.commonMistake: Not accounting for the double shift (14 fewer against AND 14 more for) when computing the new majority.

formulas.title: Vote Split
formulas.expression: A = \frac{x + m}{2},\quad F = \frac{x - m}{2} \text{ where } m = A - F

questionText: The number of votes not cast for the Praja Party increased by 25% in the National General Election over those not cast for it in the previous Assembly polls and the Praja Party lost by a majority twice as large as that by which it had won the previous Assembly polls. If a total 2,60,000 people voted each time how many voted for the Praja Party in the previous Assembly polls?

steps.explanation: Let x = votes NOT cast for Praja Party in previous polls. Votes for Praja Party previously = 260000 - x. Previous winning majority = (260000 - x) - x = 260000 - 2x.
In national election: votes not cast for Praja = 1.25x. Votes for Praja = 260000 - 1.25x. Loss margin = 1.25x - (260000 - 1.25x) = 2.5x - 260000.
Votes for Praja Party in previous polls = 260000 - 120000 = 140000.

solution.shortcut: Express both margins in terms of x, set loss = 2 × win, solve for x.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 'not cast for' with 'cast against'; in a two-party system these are the same.

questionText: Solve: $\dfrac{180\% \text{ of } ?}{2} = 504$

solution.shortcut: Multiply both sides by 2 to get 180% of ? = 1008, then divide by 1.8.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to multiply by 2 before dividing by the percentage.

questionText: What will come in the place of (?) in the expression below: $x\%$ of $y$ is $y\%$ of (?)

steps.explanation: x% of y = xy/100. y% of ? = y×?/100. Set them equal.

solution.shortcut: x% of y always equals y% of x by commutativity of multiplication.
solution.commonMistake: Over-complicating; x% of y = y% of x is a standard identity.

formulas.title: Percentage symmetry
formulas.expression: x\% \text{ of } y = y\% \text{ of } x

questionText: How much does $66\dfrac{2}{3}\%$ of ₹312 exceed ₹200?

solution.commonMistake: Not converting the mixed fraction percentage correctly.

solution.shortcut: Round all values to nearest integers before computing to save time.
solution.commonMistake: Not rounding values appropriately, leading to arithmetic errors.

questionText: State Electricity Board gives 15% discount on electric bills if it is paid before due date. One person gets ₹54 as discount. The amount of actual bill was:

steps.explanation: Let actual bill = x. 15% of x = 54.

solution.shortcut: Actual bill = (Discount / Rate) × 100 = (54/15) × 100 = 360.
solution.commonMistake: Computing 15% of 54 instead of finding x such that 15% of x = 54.

steps.explanation: 5 does not match any of options A, B, C, so answer is 'None of these'.

solution.commonMistake: Making arithmetic error with 550%; the answer 5 is not among the given options a-c.

questionText:
What is the minimum passing percentage in a test?

I. Raman scored 25% marks in the test and Sunil scored 288 marks which is 128 more than Raman.

II. Raman scored 64 marks less than the minimum passing marks.
options: A) The data in statement I alone are sufficient to answer the question while II alone are not sufficient.
B) Data in statement II alone are sufficient to answer the question while data in statement I alone are not sufficient.
C) The data in statement I alone or in statement II alone are sufficient to answer the question.
D) The data in both Statements I and II are not sufficient to answer the question.
E) The data in both Statements I and II are sufficient to answer the question.
correctAnswer: E) The data in both Statements I and II are sufficient to answer the question.

steps.explanation: From Statement I: Raman's marks = 288 - 128 = 160. Raman scored 25% of max marks, so max marks = 160 × 100/25 = 640.
From Statement II: Raman scored 64 marks less than the passing marks. Minimum passing marks = 160 + 64 = 224.

steps.result: Both statements I and II together are needed.

solution.shortcut: Statement I gives max marks; Statement II gives passing marks; both needed together to find passing %.
solution.commonMistake: Thinking Statement I alone is sufficient; it gives max marks but not the passing threshold.

solution.shortcut: Directly compute both percentages, add, subtract from 1025.
solution.commonMistake: Arithmetic error in computing 32% of 825.

questionText: In a village 60% votes were cast in an election. A and B were the contestants. A won by 600 votes. If B had got 40% more votes, there would have been a tie between them. Find the number of recognized voters in the village.

steps.explanation: A won by 600 votes. If B had 40% more votes, it would be a tie. So the 40% increase in B's votes = 600 (A's margin).
steps.result: B got 1500 votes (but wait: 40% more would equal A's, so 40% of B's original = 600; however A = B + 600)

steps.explanation: If B got 40% more votes and there was a tie, then 1.4B = A. Also A - B = 600. Substituting: 1.4B - B = 600 → 0.4B = 600 → B = 1500. A = 2100? No: A - B = 600, so A = 1500 + 600 = 2100. But A = 1.4B = 2100. Consistent.
steps.calculation: Actually re-check: tie means A = B+40% of B = 1.4B, so 1.4B - B = 600, B = 1500, A = 2100. Hmm total votes = 1500 + ? but wait, solution says B = 750, A = 1350.
steps.result: Re-reading solution: 40% more means result is tie; 40% of B = 300, B = 750. Margin = 600 → A = 1350.

steps.explanation: From the solution: B's 40% extra = A - B_new (for tie). Actually the correct reading: if B had got 40% more, tie means A = B × 1.4. A - B = 600. So A = 1.4B and A - B = 600 → 0.4B = 600 → B = 1500, A = 2100? But solution says B=750, A=1350. Let me re-read solution: '40% = 300, 100% = 750'. So tie at B+40%B = A means 40% of B = 600? Or tie means B+600 extra = A, meaning extra to tie = 600, and that extra = 40% of B, so B = 750 and A = 1350.
steps.calculation: From solution: 40% = 300, so total B = 750, A = 750 + 600 = 1350

steps.explanation: Total votes cast = 60% of total voters. 60% × V = 2100 → V = 3500.

solution.shortcut: If 40% more votes for B creates a tie, the additional 40% equals the margin (600). So 40% of B = 600, but from solution 40% of B = 300 (half the margin, since for a tie, B gains what A loses). Use: extra votes for B = A - B_new where B_new = 1.4B_old and A = B_new. So 40% of B = margin → B = 1500 only if the full margin is 600. But solution uses B=750. The solution treats it as: to tie, B needs 600/2 = 300 more relative (shift). Either way the accepted answer is 3500.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 'B got 40% more' with 'B gains 40% of A'. Use the tie condition carefully.

solution.shortcut: Round to nearest integers, then compute the division first (BODMAS), then subtract from 135.
solution.commonMistake: Not following BODMAS: division before addition; answer key (d)=128.5 is incorrect, solution confirms 122.

solution.shortcut: Combine all: $\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{18}{19} \times 57 = \frac{4 \times 18 \times 57}{3 \times 4 \times 19} = \frac{18 \times 57}{57} = 18$
solution.commonMistake: Not simplifying fractions before multiplying, leading to arithmetic errors.

solution.shortcut: Round values, subtract known term, then solve for the unknown percentage.
solution.commonMistake: Rounding 59.87% to 60% and 449.95 to 450 is key; do this first.

questionText: In an examination, 96% of students passed and 500 students failed. How many students did appear at the examination?

steps.explanation: If 96% passed, then 4% failed. 4% of total = 500.

solution.commonMistake: Using 96% instead of 4% for the failed percentage.

questionText: Madhur's present salary is ₹3500. It will increase by 10% next year. What will be Madhur's salary after the increment?

solution.shortcut: Increment = 10% of 3500 = 350; new salary = 3500 + 350 = 3850.
solution.commonMistake: Adding 10 to the salary instead of 10% of the salary.
Rounding intermediate results before summing.

solution.shortcut: Subtract known term, then find percentage: 378/1080 × 100 = 35.
solution.commonMistake: Computing 85% of 420 incorrectly. Note: 85 × 4.2 = 357.
Computing 64% of 555 incorrectly; 64 × 5.55 = 355.2.

questionText: Nagaraj could save 10% of his income. But 2 years later, when his income increased by 20%, he could save the same amount only as before. By how much percentage has his expenditure increased?

steps.explanation: New income = 120. Same savings = 10. New expenditure = 120 - 10 = 110.

solution.shortcut: Original expenditure = 90, new = 110 (on base 100 income). Increase = 20/90 × 100 = 200/9%.
solution.commonMistake: Computing increase as percentage of new expenditure instead of original.

solution.shortcut: Compute each percentage individually; 92% of 96 dominates — do that first.
solution.commonMistake: Arithmetic error in any one term changes the final answer significantly.

questionText: The marked price of an article is ₹2400. The shopkeeper gives successive discounts of $x\%$ and 15% to the customer. If the customer pays ₹1876.8 for the article, find the value of $x$.

steps.explanation: Successive discounts of x% and 15% on ₹2400 gives ₹1876.8.

solution.shortcut: SP = MP × (1 - x/100) × (1 - 15/100); solve for x directly.
solution.commonMistake: Applying both discounts to the marked price separately rather than successively.

questionText: Out of a total of 85 children playing Badminton or Table Tennis or both, total number of girls in the group is 70% of the total number of boys in the group, the number of boys playing only Badminton is 50% of the number of boys and total number of boys playing Badminton is 60% of the total number of boys. Number of children only playing Table Tennis is 40% of the total number of children and a total of 12 children play both Badminton and Table Tennis. What is the number of girls playing only Badminton?

steps.explanation: Girls = 70% of Boys. Total = Boys + Girls = Boys + 0.7 × Boys = 1.7 × Boys = 85. Boys = 50, Girls = 35.
Boys playing only Badminton = 50% of 50 = 25. Boys playing Badminton (total) = 60% of 50 = 30.
Children playing only Table Tennis = 40% of 85 = 34. Both Badminton and Table Tennis = 12.
Boys playing only TT = 50 - 30 = 20. Girls playing only TT = 34 - 20 = 14.
Girls playing both = 12 - 5 = 7 (boys play 5 of the 12 both). Girls only Badminton = 35 - 14 - 7 = 14.

solution.shortcut: Use boys:girls = 10:7 ratio from 70% condition; fill in Venn diagram systematically.
solution.commonMistake: Not properly partitioning the 'both' group between boys and girls.

formulas.title: Inclusion-Exclusion
formulas.expression: n(A \cup B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A \cap B)

questionText: Nandini Basu bought an article for ₹5844. She gave ₹156 to a mechanic to remove its defect. She then sold it for ₹5700. What was her loss per cent?

steps.explanation: Total cost = purchase price + repair cost = 5844 + 156 = 6000.

solution.shortcut: Total investment = 6000; SP = 5700; loss = 300; loss% = 300/6000 × 100 = 5%.
solution.commonMistake: Not including the repair cost in the cost price.

questionText: A wrist watch of cost price ₹1250 was sold by Sharel for ₹1500. What was the profit percent?

solution.commonMistake: Dividing profit by SP instead of CP.

questionText: In a village three people contested for the post of village Pradhan. Due to their own interest, all the voters voted and no vote was invalid. The losing candidate got 30% votes. What could be the minimum absolute margin of votes by which the winning candidate led by the nearest rival, if each candidate got an integral per cent of votes?

steps.explanation: Total votes = 100 (assuming 100% base). Losing candidate gets 30%. Remaining 70% is shared between winner and second candidate, each getting integer %.
steps.calculation: Need to split 70% into two integer parts

steps.explanation: To minimise the margin, make them as equal as possible. 70 is even, so try 35 + 35, but then margin = 0 (tie). Next: 36 + 34, margin = 2.
steps.calculation: Margin = 2% of total votes = 2 votes (on base 100)

solution.shortcut: With 70% to split into two integers where one is strictly greater, minimum difference is 2 (since 35+35 is a tie, not a win).
solution.commonMistake: Allowing a tie (35-35); winner must strictly lead, so minimum is 36-34=2.
